rawText: A mixed-methods study of more than 7,600 US women aged 35 and older, published 15 July 2026 in Menopause (journal of The Menopause Society), found 34% reported being unsure of their reproductive stage, rising to 42% among women aged 40–44 and 37% among those with severe symptom burden. Free-text responses attributed the uncertainty to symptom confusion and attribution (56%), knowledge gaps and information-seeking (28%) and barriers to confirmation and care (16%); women aged 35–39 were more likely to cite knowledge gaps while healthcare barriers peaked at 40–44. The paper notes there is no laboratory test or biomarker to definitively determine perimenopause stage, and that 'there has been a propagation of misinformation about menopause and symptom management in recent years'. · ANGLES: (1) The buyer moment — a third of the demographic sold stage-specific formulations cannot confirm they are in the stage. (2) No biomarker exists, so what is a 'perimenopause formula' formulated against? (3) The 56% who cannot tell which symptom belongs to what — an app resolver route: pain point, proof of the confusing environment, useful check, app doing the check, before-you-buy CTA. · APP CONNECTION: paste product — the buyer is stuck between symptom uncertainty and stage-specific marketing; the app checks what the product's claim rests on rather than requiring the buyer to self-diagnose first. · SECONDARY: https://bioengineer.org/many-women-still-confused-by-perimenopause/ (carries the release verbatim, grade B); doi 10.1097/GME.0000000000000002837 (Menopause, grade A — cited in both write-ups, not independently fetched) · SOURCE GRADE: A study reported via grade B coverage. · RISK NOTE: US sample — do not present the percentages as UK figures. The study does not mention or criticise any supplement or brand; the gap is structural and must not be framed as an accusation against any named party.
